translation.original_text: <sup>501</sup>diversas obitumque ducum, quos aequore toto <sup>502</sup>inque vicem nunc Turnus agit, nunc Troius heros, <sup>503</sup>expediat? Tanton placuit concurrere motu, <sup>504</sup>Iuppiter, aeterna gentis in pace futuras? <sup>505</sup>Aeneas Rutulum Sucronem, ea prima ruentis <sup>506</sup>pugna loco statuit Teucros, haud multa morantem <sup>507</sup>excipit in latus et, qua fata celerrima, crudum <sup>508</sup>transadigit costas et cratis pectoris ensem. <sup>509</sup>Turnus equo deiectum Amycum fratremque Dioren, <sup>510</sup>congressus pedes, hunc venientem cuspide longa, <sup>511</sup>hunc mucrone ferit curruque abscisa duorum <sup>512</sup>suspendit capita et rorantia sanguine portat. <sup>513</sup>Ille Talon Tanaimque neci fortemque Cethegum, <sup>514</sup>tris uno congressu, et maestum mittit Oniten, <sup>515</sup>nomen Echionium matrisque genus Peridiae, <sup>516</sup>hic fratres Lycia missos et Apollinis agris <sup>517</sup>et iuvenem exosum nequiquam bella Menoeten, <sup>518</sup>Arcada, piscosae cui circum flumina Lernae <sup>519</sup>ars fuerat pauperque domus nec nota potentum <sup>520</sup>munera conductaque pater tellure serebat. <sup>521</sup>Ac velut immissi diversis partibus ignes <sup>522</sup>arentem in silvam et virgulta sonantia lauro, <sup>523</sup>aut ubi decursu rapido de montibus altis <sup>524</sup>dant sonitum spumosi amnes et in aequora currunt <sup>525</sup>quisque suum populatus iter: non segnius ambo
translation.text:
Thus, when a fearful stag is clos’d around With crimson toils, or in a river found, High on the bank the deep-mouth’d hound appears, Still opening, following still, where’er he steers; The persecuted creature, to and fro, Turns here and there, to scape his Umbrian foe: Steep is th’ ascent, and, if he gains the land, The purple death is pitch’d along the strand. His eager foe, determin’d to the chase, Stretch’d at his length, gains ground at ev’ry pace; Now to his beamy head he makes his way, And now he holds, or thinks he holds, his prey: Just at the pinch, the stag springs out with fear; He bites the wind, and fills his sounding jaws with air: The rocks, the lakes, the meadows ring with cries; The mortal tumult mounts, and thunders in the skies. Thus flies the Daunian prince, and, flying, blames His tardy troops, and, calling by their names, Demands his trusty sword. The Trojan threats The realm with ruin, and their ancient seats To lay in ashes, if they dare supply With arms or aid his vanquish’d enemy: Thus menacing, he still pursues the course, With vigour, tho’ diminish’d of his force. Ten times already round the listed place One chief had fled, and t’ other giv’n the chase: No trivial prize is play’d; for on the life Or death of Turnus now depends the strife.

Within the space, an olive tree had stood, A sacred shade, a venerable wood, For vows to Faunus paid, the Latins’ guardian god. Here hung the vests, and tablets were engrav’d, Of sinking mariners from shipwreck sav’d. With heedless hands the Trojans fell’d the tree, To make the ground enclos’d for combat free. Deep in the root, whether by fate, or chance, Or erring haste, the Trojan drove his lance; Then stoop’d, and tugg’d with force immense, to free Th’ incumber’d spear from the tenacious tree; That, whom his fainting limbs pursued in vain, His flying weapon might from far attain.

Confus’d with fear, bereft of human aid, Then Turnus to the gods, and first to Faunus pray’d: “O Faunus, pity! and thou Mother Earth, Where I thy foster son receiv’d my birth, Hold fast the steel! If my religious hand Your plant has honour’d, which your foes profan’d, Propitious hear my pious pray’r!” He said, Nor with successless vows invok’d their aid. Th’ incumbent hero wrench’d, and pull’d, and strain’d; But still the stubborn earth the steel detain’d. Juturna took her time; and, while in vain He strove, assum’d Meticus’ form again, And, in that imitated shape, restor’d To the despairing prince his Daunian sword. The Queen of Love, who, with disdain and grief, Saw the bold nymph afford this prompt relief, T’ assert her offspring with a greater deed, From the tough root the ling’ring weapon freed.

translation.original_text: <sup>526</sup>Aeneas Turnusque ruunt per proelia; nunc nunc <sup>527</sup>fluctuat ira intus, rumpuntur nescia vinci <sup>528</sup>pectora, nunc totis in volnera viribus itur. <sup>529</sup>Murranum hic, atavos et avorum antiqua sonantem <sup>530</sup>nomina per regesque actum genus omne Latinos, <sup>531</sup>praecipitem scopulo atque ingentis turbine saxi <sup>532</sup>excutit effunditque solo: hunc lora et iuga subter <sup>533</sup>provolvere rotae, crebro super ungula pulsu <sup>534</sup>incita nec domini memorum proculcat equorum. <sup>535</sup>Ille ruenti Hyllo animisque immane frementi <sup>536</sup>occurrit telumque aurata ad tempora torquet: <sup>537</sup>olli per galeam fixo stetit hasta cerebro. <sup>538</sup>Dextera nec tua te, Graium fortissime Cretheu <sup>539</sup>eripuit Turno. Nec di texere Cupencum <sup>540</sup>Aenea veniente sui: dedit obvia ferro <sup>541</sup>pectora, nec misero clipei mora profuit aerei. <sup>542</sup>Te quoque Laurentes viderunt, Aeole, campi <sup>543</sup>oppetere et late terram consternere tergo. <sup>544</sup>Occidis, Argivae quem non potuere phalanges <sup>545</sup>sternere nec Priami regnorum eversor Achilles; <sup>546</sup>hic tibi mortis erant metae: domus alta sub Ida, <sup>547</sup>Lyrnesi domus alta, solo Laurente sepulchrum. <sup>548</sup>Totae adeo conversae acies, omnesque Latini, <sup>549</sup>omnes Dardanidae, Mnestheus acerque Serestus <sup>550</sup>et Messapus equum domitor et fortis Asilas
translation.text:
Once more erect, the rival chiefs advance: One trusts the sword, and one the pointed lance; And both resolv’d alike to try their fatal chance.

Meantime imperial Jove to Juno spoke, Who from a shining cloud beheld the shock: “What new arrest, O Queen of Heav’n, is sent To stop the Fates now lab’ring in th’ event? What farther hopes are left thee to pursue? Divine Aeneas, (and thou know’st it too,) Foredoom’d, to these celestial seats are due. What more attempts for Turnus can be made, That thus thou ling’rest in this lonely shade? Is it becoming of the due respect And awful honour of a god elect, A wound unworthy of our state to feel, Patient of human hands and earthly steel? Or seems it just, the sister should restore A second sword, when one was lost before, And arm a conquer’d wretch against his conqueror? For what, without thy knowledge and avow, Nay more, thy dictate, durst Juturna do? At last, in deference to my love, forbear To lodge within thy soul this anxious care; Reclin’d upon my breast, thy grief unload: Who should relieve the goddess, but the god? Now all things to their utmost issue tend, Push’d by the Fates to their appointed end. While leave was giv’n thee, and a lawful hour For vengeance, wrath, and unresisted pow’r, Toss’d on the seas, thou couldst thy foes distress, And, driv’n ashore, with hostile arms oppress; Deform the royal house; and, from the side Of the just bridegroom, tear the plighted bride: Now cease at my command.” The Thund’rer said; And, with dejected eyes, this answer Juno made: “Because your dread decree too well I knew, From Turnus and from earth unwilling I withdrew. Else should you not behold me here, alone, Involv’d in empty clouds, my friends bemoan, But, girt with vengeful flames, in open sight Engag’d against my foes in mortal fight. ’Tis true, Juturna mingled in the strife By my command, to save her brother’s life, At least to try; but, by the Stygian lake, (The most religious oath the gods can take,) With this restriction, not to bend the bow, Or toss the spear, or trembling dart to throw. And now, resign’d to your superior might, And tir’d with fruitless toils, I loathe the fight. This let me beg (and this no fates withstand) Both for myself and for your father’s land, That, when the nuptial bed shall bind the peace, (Which I, since you ordain, consent to bless,) The laws of either nation be the same; But let the Latins still retain their name, Speak the same language which they spoke before, Wear the same habits which their grandsires wore. Call them not Trojans: perish the renown And name of Troy, with that detested town. Latium be Latium still; let Alba reign And Rome’s immortal majesty remain.”

Then thus the founder of mankind replies (Unruffled was his front, serene his eyes) “Can Saturn’s issue, and heav’n’s other heir, Such endless anger in her bosom bear? Be mistress, and your full desires obtain; But quench the choler you foment in vain. From ancient blood th’ Ausonian people sprung, Shall keep their name, their habit, and their tongue. The Trojans to their customs shall be tied: I will, myself, their common rites provide; The natives shall command, the foreigners subside. All shall be Latium; Troy without a name; And her lost sons forget from whence they came. From blood so mix’d, a pious race shall flow, Equal to gods, excelling all below. No nation more respect to you shall pay, Or greater off’rings on your altars lay.” Juno consents, well pleas’d that her desires Had found success, and from the cloud retires.

translation.original_text: <sup>551</sup>Tuscorumque phalanx Evandrique Arcades alae, <sup>552</sup>pro se quisque viri summa nituntur opum vi: <sup>553</sup>nec mora nec requies, vasto certamine tendunt. <sup>554</sup>Hic mentem Aeneae genetrix pulcherrima misit, <sup>555</sup>iret ut ad muros urbique adverteret agmen <sup>556</sup>ocius et subita turbaret clade Latinos. <sup>557</sup>Ille ut vestigans diversa per agmina Turnum <sup>558</sup>huc atque huc acies circumtulit, aspicit urbem <sup>559</sup>immunem tanti belli atque impune quietam. <sup>560</sup>Continuo pugnae adcendit maioris imago: <sup>561</sup>Mnesthea Sergestumque vocat fortemque Serestum <sup>562</sup>ductores tumulumque capit, quo cetera Teucrum <sup>563</sup>concurrit legio nec scuta aut spicula densi <sup>564</sup>deponunt. Celso medius stans aggere fatur: <sup>565</sup>Nequa meis esto dictis mora; Iuppiter hac stat, <sup>566</sup>neu quis ob inceptum subitum mihi segnior ito. <sup>567</sup>Urbem hodie, causam belli, regna ipsa Latini, <sup>568</sup>ni frenum accipere et victi parere fatentur, <sup>569</sup>eruam et aequa solo fumantia culmina ponam. <sup>570</sup>Scilicet exspectem, libeat dum proelia Turno <sup>571</sup>nostra pati rursusque velit concurrere victus? <sup>572</sup>Hoc caput, O cives, haec belli summa nefandi: <sup>573</sup>ferte faces propere foedusque reposcite flammis. <sup>574</sup>Dixerat, atque animis pariter certantibus omnes <sup>575</sup>dant cuneum densaque ad muros mole feruntur.
translation.text:
The peace thus made, the Thund’rer next prepares To force the wat’ry goddess from the wars. Deep in the dismal regions void of light, Three daughters at a birth were born to Night: These their brown mother, brooding on her care, Indued with windy wings to flit in air, With serpents girt alike, and crown’d with hissing hair. In heav’n the Dirae call’d, and still at hand, Before the throne of angry Jove they stand, His ministers of wrath, and ready still The minds of mortal men with fears to fill, Whene’er the moody sire, to wreak his hate On realms or towns deserving of their fate, Hurls down diseases, death and deadly care, And terrifies the guilty world with war. One sister plague if these from heav’n he sent, To fright Juturna with a dire portent. The pest comes whirling down: by far more slow Springs the swift arrow from the Parthian bow, Or Cydon yew, when, traversing the skies, And drench’d in pois’nous juice, the sure destruction flies. With such a sudden and unseen a flight Shot thro’ the clouds the daughter of the night. Soon as the field inclos’d she had in view, And from afar her destin’d quarry knew, Contracted, to the boding bird she turns, Which haunts the ruin’d piles and hallow’d urns, And beats about the tombs with nightly wings, Where songs obscene on sepulchers she sings. Thus lessen’d in her form, with frightful cries The Fury round unhappy Turnus flies, Flaps on his shield, and flutters o’er his eyes.

A lazy chillness crept along his blood; Chok’d was his voice; his hair with horror stood. Juturna from afar beheld her fly, And knew th’ ill omen, by her screaming cry And stridor of her wings. Amaz’d with fear, Her beauteous breast she beat, and rent her flowing hair.

“Ah me!” she cries, “in this unequal strife What can thy sister more to save thy life? Weak as I am, can I, alas! contend In arms with that inexorable fiend? Now, now, I quit the field! forbear to fright My tender soul, ye baleful birds of night; The lashing of your wings I know too well, The sounding flight, and fun’ral screams of hell! These are the gifts you bring from haughty Jove, The worthy recompense of ravish’d love! Did he for this exempt my life from fate? O hard conditions of immortal state, Tho’ born to death, not privileg’d to die, But forc’d to bear impos’d eternity! Take back your envious bribes, and let me go Companion to my brother’s ghost below! The joys are vanish’d: nothing now remains, Of life immortal, but immortal pains. What earth will open her devouring womb, To rest a weary goddess in the tomb!” She drew a length of sighs; nor more she said, But in her azure mantle wrapp’d her head, Then plung’d into her stream, with deep despair, And her last sobs came bubbling up in air.

Now stern Aeneas waves his weighty spear Against his foe, and thus upbraids his fear: “What farther subterfuge can Turnus find? What empty hopes are harbour’d in his mind? ’Tis not thy swiftness can secure thy flight; Not with their feet, but hands, the valiant fight. Vary thy shape in thousand forms, and dare What skill and courage can attempt in war; Wish for the wings of winds, to mount the sky; Or hid, within the hollow earth to lie!” The champion shook his head, and made this short reply: “No threats of thine my manly mind can move; ’Tis hostile heav’n I dread, and partial Jove.” He said no more, but, with a sigh, repress’d The mighty sorrow in his swelling breast.

translation.original_text: <sup>576</sup>Scalae improviso subitusque apparuit ignis. <sup>577</sup>Discurrunt alii ad portas primosque trucidant, <sup>578</sup>ferrum alii torquent et obumbrant aethera telis. <sup>579</sup>Ipse inter primos dextram sub moenia tendit <sup>580</sup>Aeneas magnaque incusat voce Latinum <sup>581</sup>testaturque deos, iterum se ad proelia cogi, <sup>582</sup>bis iam Italos hostis, haec altera foedera rumpi. <sup>583</sup>Exoritur trepidos inter discordia civis: <sup>584</sup>urbem alii reserare iubent et pandere portas <sup>585</sup>Dardanidis ipsumque trahunt in moenia regem, <sup>586</sup>arma ferunt alii et pergunt defendere muros. <sup>587</sup>Inclusas ut cum latebroso in pumice pastor <sup>588</sup>vestigavit apes fumoque implevit amaro: <sup>589</sup>illae intus trepidae rerum per cerea castra <sup>590</sup>discurrunt magnisque acuunt stridoribus iras; <sup>591</sup>volvitur ater odor tectis, tum murmure caeco <sup>592</sup>intus saxa sonant, vacuas it fumus ad auras. <sup>593</sup>Accidit haec fessis etiam fortuna Latinis, <sup>594</sup>quae totam luctu concussit funditus urbem. <sup>595</sup>Regina ut tectis venientem prospicit hostem, <sup>596</sup>incessi muros, ignis ad tecta volare, <sup>597</sup>nusquam acies contra Rutulas, nulla agmina Turni: <sup>598</sup>infelix pugnae iuvenem in certamine credit <sup>599</sup>exstinctum et, subito mentem turbata dolore, <sup>600</sup>se causam clamat crimenque caputque malorum,
translation.text:
Then, as he roll’d his troubled eyes around, An antique stone he saw, the common bound Of neighb’ring fields, and barrier of the ground; So vast, that twelve strong men of modern days Th’ enormous weight from earth could hardly raise. He heav’d it at a lift, and, pois’d on high, Ran stagg’ring on against his enemy, But so disorder’d, that he scarcely knew His way, or what unwieldly weight he threw. His knocking knees are bent beneath the load, And shiv’ring cold congeals his vital blood. The stone drops from his arms, and, falling short For want of vigour, mocks his vain effort. And as, when heavy sleep has clos’d the sight, The sickly fancy labours in the night; We seem to run; and, destitute of force, Our sinking limbs forsake us in the course: In vain we heave for breath; in vain we cry; The nerves, unbrac’d, their usual strength deny; And on the tongue the falt’ring accents die: So Turnus far’d; whatever means he tried, All force of arms and points of art employ’d, The Fury flew athwart, and made th’ endeavor void.

A thousand various thoughts his soul confound; He star’d about, nor aid nor issue found; His own men stop the pass, and his own walls surround. Once more he pauses, and looks out again, And seeks the goddess charioteer in vain. Trembling he views the thund’ring chief advance, And brandishing aloft the deadly lance: Amaz’d he cow’rs beneath his conqu’ring foe, Forgets to ward, and waits the coming blow. Astonish’d while he stands, and fix’d with fear, Aim’d at his shield he sees th’ impending spear.

The hero measur’d first, with narrow view, The destin’d mark; and, rising as he threw, With its full swing the fatal weapon flew. Not with less rage the rattling thunder falls, Or stones from batt’ring-engines break the walls: Swift as a whirlwind, from an arm so strong, The lance drove on, and bore the death along. Naught could his sev’nfold shield the prince avail, Nor aught, beneath his arms, the coat of mail: It pierc’d thro’ all, and with a grisly wound Transfix’d his thigh, and doubled him to ground. With groans the Latins rend the vaulted sky: Woods, hills, and valleys, to the voice reply.

translation.original_text: <sup>601</sup>multaque per maestum demens effata furorem <sup>602</sup>purpureos moritura manu discindit amictus <sup>603</sup>et nodum informis leti trabe nectit ab alta. <sup>604</sup>Quam cladem miserae postquam accepere Latinae, <sup>605</sup>filia prima manu flavos Lavinia crinis <sup>606</sup>et roseas laniata genas, tum cetera circum <sup>607</sup>turba furit: resonant late plangoribus aedes. <sup>608</sup>Hinc totam infelix volgatur fama per urbem. <sup>609</sup>Demittunt mentes; it scissa veste Latinus, <sup>610</sup>coniugis attonitus fatis urbisque ruina, <sup>611</sup>canitiem immundo perfusam pulvere turpans. <sup>612</sup>Multaque se incusat, qui non acceperit ante <sup>613</sup>Dardanium Aenean generumque adsciverit ultro. <sup>614</sup>Interea extremo bellator in aequore Turnus <sup>615</sup>palantis sequitur paucos iam segnior atque <sup>616</sup>iam minus atque minus successu laetus equorum <sup>617</sup>attulit hunc illi caecis terroribus aura <sup>618</sup>commixtum clamorem adrectasque impulit aures <sup>619</sup>confusae sonus urbis et inlaetabile murmur. <sup>620</sup>Ei mihi! Quid tanto turbantur moenia luctu? <sup>621</sup>Quisve ruit tantus diversa clamor ab urbe? <sup>622</sup>Sic ait adductisque amens subsistit habenis. <sup>623</sup>Atque huic, in faciem soror ut conversa Metisci <sup>624</sup>aurigae currumque et equos et lora regebat, <sup>625</sup>talibus occurrit dictis: Hac, Turne, sequamur
translation.text:
Now low on earth the lofty chief is laid, With eyes cast upward, and with arms display’d, And, recreant, thus to the proud victor pray’d: “I know my death deserv’d, nor hope to live: Use what the gods and thy good fortune give. Yet think, O think, if mercy may be shown, Thou hadst a father once, and hast a son. Pity my sire, now sinking to the grave; And for Anchises’ sake old Daunus save! Or, if thy vow’d revenge pursue my death, Give to my friends my body void of breath! The Latian chiefs have seen me beg my life; Thine is the conquest, thine the royal wife: Against a yielded man, ’tis mean ignoble strife.”

In deep suspense the Trojan seem’d to stand, And, just prepar’d to strike, repress’d his hand. He roll’d his eyes, and ev’ry moment felt His manly soul with more compassion melt; When, casting down a casual glance, he spied The golden belt that glitter’d on his side, The fatal spoils which haughty Turnus tore From dying Pallas, and in triumph wore. Then, rous’d anew to wrath, he loudly cries (Flames, while he spoke, came flashing from his eyes) “Traitor, dost thou, dost thou to grace pretend, Clad, as thou art, in trophies of my friend? To his sad soul a grateful off’ring go! ’Tis Pallas, Pallas gives this deadly blow.” He rais’d his arm aloft, and, at the word, Deep in his bosom drove the shining sword. The streaming blood distain’d his arms around; And the disdainful soul came rushing through the wound.

translation.original_text: <sup>626</sup>Troiugenas, qua prima viam victoria pandit; <sup>627</sup>sunt alii, qui tecta manu defendere possint. <sup>628</sup>Ingruit Aeneas Italis et proelia miscet: <sup>629</sup>et nos saeva manu mittamus funera Teucris. <sup>630</sup>Nec numero inferior pugnae nec honore recedes. <sup>631</sup>Turnus ad haec: <sup>632</sup>O soror, et dudum adgnovi, cum prima per artem <sup>633</sup>foedera turbasti teque haec in bella dedisti, <sup>634</sup>et nunc nequiquam fallis dea. Sed quis Olympo <sup>635</sup>demissam tantos voluit te ferre labores? <sup>636</sup>An fratris miseri letum ut crudele videres? <sup>637</sup>Nam quid ago? Aut quae iam spondet Fortuna salutem? <sup>638</sup>Vidi oculos ante ipse meos me voce vocantem <sup>639</sup>Murranum, quo non superat mihi carior alter, <sup>640</sup>oppetere ingentem atque ingenti volnere victum. <sup>641</sup>Occidit infelix nostrum ne dedecus Ufens <sup>642</sup>adspiceret; Teucri potiuntur corpore et armis. <sup>643</sup>Exscindine domos (id rebus defuit unum) <sup>644</sup>perpetiar, dextra nec Drancis dicta refellam? <sup>645</sup>Terga dabo et Turnum fugientem haec terra videbit? <sup>646</sup>Usque adeone mori miserum est? Vos O mihi Manes <sup>647</sup>este boni, quoniam superis aversa voluntas! <sup>648</sup>Sancta ad vos anima atque istius nescia culpae <sup>649</sup>descendam, magnorum haud umquam indignus avorum. <sup>650</sup>Vix ea fatus erat: medios volat ecce per hostis

translation.original_text: <sup>651</sup>vectus equo spumante Saces, adversa sagitta <sup>652</sup>saucius ora, ruitque implorans nomine Turnum: <sup>653</sup>Turne, in te suprema salus: miserere tuorum. <sup>654</sup>Fulminat Aeneas armis summasque minatur <sup>655</sup>deiecturum arces Italum exscidioque daturum; <sup>656</sup>iamque faces ad tecta volant. In te ora Latini, <sup>657</sup>in te oculos referunt; mussat rex ipse Latinus, <sup>658</sup>quos generos vocet aut quae sese ad foedera flectat. <sup>659</sup>Praeterea regina, tui fidissima, dextra <sup>660</sup>occidit ipsa sua lucemque exterrita fugit. <sup>661</sup>Soli pro portis Messapus et acer Atinas <sup>662</sup>sustentant aciem. Circum hos utrimque phalanges <sup>663</sup>stant densae, strictisque seges mucronibus horret <sup>664</sup>ferrea: tu currum deserto in gramine versas. <sup>665</sup>Obstipuit varia confusus imagine rerum <sup>666</sup>Turnus et obtutu tacito stetit. Aestuat ingens <sup>667</sup>uno in corde pudor mixtoque insania luctu <sup>668</sup>et furiis agitatus amor et conscia virtus. <sup>669</sup>Ut primum discussae umbrae et lux reddita menti, <sup>670</sup>ardentis oculorum orbes ad moenia torsit <sup>671</sup>turbidus eque rotis magnam respexit ad urbem. <sup>672</sup>Ecce autem flammis inter tabulata volutus <sup>673</sup>ad caelum undabat vortex turrimque tenebat, <sup>674</sup>turrim, compactis trabibus quam eduxerat ipse <sup>675</sup>subdideratque rotas pontisque instraverat altos.

translation.original_text: <sup>676</sup>Iam iam fata, soror, superant; absiste morari; <sup>677</sup>quo deus et quo dura vocat Fortuna, sequamur. <sup>678</sup>Stat conferre manum Aeneae, stat quidquid acerbi est <sup>679</sup>morte pati; neque me indecorem, germana, videbis <sup>680</sup>amplius. Hunc, oro, sine me furere ante furorem. <sup>681</sup>Dixit et e curru saltum dedit ocius arvis <sup>682</sup>perque hostis, per tela ruit maestamque sororem <sup>683</sup>deserit ac rapido cursu media agmina rumpit. <sup>684</sup>Ac veluti montis saxum de vertice praeceps <sup>685</sup>cum ruit avulsum vento, seu turbidus imber <sup>686</sup>proluit aut annis solvit sublapsa vetustas; <sup>687</sup>fertur in abruptum magno mons improbus actu <sup>688</sup>exsultatque solo, silvas armenta virosque <sup>689</sup>involvens secum: disiecta per agmina Turnus <sup>690</sup>sic urbis ruit ad muros, ubi plurima fuso <sup>691</sup>sanguine terra madet striduntque hastilibus aurae, <sup>692</sup>significatque manu et magno simul incipit ore: <sup>693</sup>Parcite iam, Rutuli, et vos tela inhibete, Latini; <sup>694</sup>quaecumque est Fortuna, mea est: me verius unum <sup>695</sup>pro vobis foedus luere et decernere ferro. <sup>696</sup>Discessere omnes medii spatiumque dedere. <sup>697</sup>At pater Aeneas audito nomine Turni <sup>698</sup>deserit et muros et summas deserit arces <sup>699</sup>praecipitatque moras omnis, opera omnia rumpit, <sup>700</sup>laetitia exsultans, horrendumque intonat armis;
